concepts.title: Standard forms
concepts.conceptBody:
Tangent

In Geometry the tangent at any point of a circle is defined to be a straight line which meets the circle there, but, being produced, does not cut it; this tangent is shown to be always perpendicular to the radius drawn from the Centre to the point of contact.

Different forms of the equation of tangent

1. Point form 

The equation of the tangent at a point $P\left( x,y \right)$ to a circle ${{x}^{2}}+{{y}^{2}}+2gx+2fy+c=0$ is

$\text{x}{{\text{x}}_{1}}+\text{y}{{\text{y}}_{1}}+\text{g}\left( \text{x}+{{\text{x}}_{1}} \right)+\text{f}\left( \text{y}+{{\text{y}}_{1}} \right)+\text{c}=0$

for equation of circle in standard form the equation becomes

$\text{x}{{\text{x}}_{1}}+\text{y}{{\text{y}}_{1}}={{\text{a}}^{2}}$

Note : For equation of tangent of circle at $\left( {{\text{x}}_{1}},{{\text{y}}_{1}} \right)$, substitute $\text{x}{{\text{x}}_{1}}$ for ${{\text{x}}^{2}},\text{y}{{\text{y}}^{1}}$

${{\text{y}}^{2}},\frac{\text{x}+{{\text{x}}_{1}}}{2} \text{ for } \text{ x},\frac{\text{y}+{{\text{y}}_{1}}}{2} \text{ for } \text{ y } \text{ and } \frac{\text{x}{{\text{y}}_{1}}+{{\text{x}}_{1}}\text{y}}{2} \text{ for } \text{ xy } \text{ and } \text{ keep } \text{ the } \text{ constant } \text{ as } \text{ such }$

2. Parametric form: 

The equation of tangent to the circle ${{x}^{2}}+{{y}^{2}}={{a}^{2}}$ at the point $(a\text{cos}\theta $, a $\text{sin}\theta )$ is

$x\text{cos}\theta +y\text{sin}\theta =a$

Note: The equation of the tangent to the circle ${{(x-a)}^{2}}+{{(y-b)}^{2}}={{r}^{2}}$ at the point $(a+r\text{cos}$ $\theta ,b+r\text{sin}\theta )$ is

$\left( x-a \right)\text{cos}\theta +\left( y-b \right)\text{sin}\theta =r.$

3. Slope form: 

The equation of a tangent of slope $m$ to the circle ${{x}^{2}}+{{y}^{2}}={{a}^{2}}$ is $y=mx\pm a\sqrt{\left( 1+{{m}^{2}} \right)}$ and the co-ordinates of the point of contact are $\left( \pm \frac{\text{am}}{\sqrt{\left( 1+{{\text{m}}^{2}} \right)}},\mp \frac{\text{a}}{\sqrt{\left( 1+{{\text{m}}^{2}} \right)}} \right)$

concepts.title: Length of Tangent
concepts.conceptBody:
Length of tangent, normal, subtangent and subnormal 

Let ${{y}_{1}}=\frac{dy}{dx}=\text{tan}\psi $

1. Length of tangent at a point : The portion of the tangent which is intercepted between the point of contact $\text{P}$ and $\text{x}$-axis is called the length of the tangent at $\text{P}$.

 

In the given figure, length of tangent at $\text{P}$

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & ~=\text{PL}=\sqrt{\text{L}{{\text{M}}^{2}}+\text{P}{{\text{M}}^{2}}} \\ {} & ~=\sqrt{{{(\text{ycot}\psi )}^{2}}+{{\text{y}}^{2}}} \\ {} & ~=\left| \text{y} \right|\sqrt{\text{co}{{\text{t}}^{2}}\psi +1} \\ {} & ~=\left| \text{y} \right|\sqrt{\frac{1}{\text{y}_{1}^{2}}+1}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left[ \therefore \text{tan }\!\!\Psi\!\!\text{ }={{\text{y}}_{1}} \right] \\ {} & ~=\left| \frac{\text{y}}{{{\text{y}}_{1}}} \right|\sqrt{1+\text{y}_{1}^{2}} \\ \end{array}$

2. Subtangent at a point: Projection of LP on the positive direction of $x$-axis is called the subtangent at point $P$.

Subtangent at $\mathbf{P}=\text{LM}=\text{ycot}\psi =\frac{\text{y}}{{{\text{y}}_{1}}}$ , subtangent at a point may be positive or negative.

Thus the subtangent at $\text{P}$ is positive or negative according as $\text{M}$ lies on the right or left side of $\text{L}$.

Length of subtangent at $\text{P}=\left| \frac{\text{y}}{{{\text{y}}_{1}}} \right|$.

3. Length of normal at a point : The portion of the normal at $\text{P}$ intercepted between the point $\text{P}$ and $\text{x}$-axis is called the length of the normal at $\text{P}$.

In the given figure, length of normal at $\text{P}$

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & ~=\text{PK}=\sqrt{\text{P}{{\text{M}}^{2}}+\text{M}{{\text{K}}^{2}}} \\ {} & ~=\sqrt{{{\text{y}}^{2}}+{{(\text{ytan}\psi )}^{2}}} \\ {} & ~=\left| \text{y} \right|\sqrt{1+\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{2}}\psi }=\left| \text{y} \right|\sqrt{1+\text{y}_{1}^{2}} \\ \end{array}$

4. Subtangent at a point : The projection of $\text{PK}$ on the $\text{x}$-axis is called the subtangent at $\text{P}$ Subtangent at $\text{P}=\text{MK}=\text{ytan }\!\!\Psi\!\!\text{ }={{\text{y}}_{1}}$

subtangent at a point may be positive or negative. The subtangent at $\text{P}$ is positive or negative according as $\text{K}$ lies on the right or left side of $\text{M}$.

concepts.title: Equation of pair of tangents
concepts.conceptBody:
Equation of pair of tangents 

The equation of pair of pair of tangents drawn

from an external point $P\left( {{x}_{1}}{{y}_{1}} \right)$ to the circle ${{x}^{2}}+{{y}^{2}}={{a}^{2}}$ is given by

$\left( {{x}^{2}}+{{y}^{2}}-{{a}^{2}} \right)\left( x_{1}^{2}+y_{1}^{2}-{{a}^{2}} \right)=\left( {{x}_{1}}+{{y}_{1}}-{{a}^{2}} \right)$

 

OR 

we can say $\mathbf{S}{{\mathbf{S}}_{1}}={{\mathbf{T}}^{2}}$

where $\text{S}\equiv $ equation of circle

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & {{\text{S}}_{1}}\equiv  \text{ value }\text{ after }\text{ putting } \left( {{\text{x}}_{1}}{{\text{y}}_{1}} \right) \text{ in }\text{ equation }\text{ of }\text{ circle }  \\ {} & \text{T}\equiv  \text{ equation }\text{ of }\text{ tangent }\text{ at }\text{ point } \left( {{\text{x}}_{1}}{{\text{y}}_{1}} \right) \\ \end{array}$

Note: If the equation of circle is given in general form then also the condition $\text{S}{{\text{S}}_{1}}={{\text{T}}^{2}}$ is valued for the equation of circle, ${{x}^{2}}+{{y}^{2}}+2gx+2fy+c=0$, the equation becomes

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} \left( {{x}^{2}}+{{y}^{2}}+2gx+ \right. & 2fy+c)\left( x_{1}^{2}+y_{1}^{2}+2{{g}_{1}}+2f{{y}_{1}}+c \right) \\ = & {{\left( {{x}_{1}}+{{y}_{1}}+g\left( x+{{x}_{1}} \right)+f\left( y+{{y}_{1}} \right) \right)}^{2}}+c \\ \end{array}$

Note that the equation of tangent is also with respect to the general equation of circle.

Director circle 

The locus of the point of intersection of two perpendicular tangents to a given conic is known as its director circle.

The equation of the director circle of the circle ${{x}^{2}}+{{y}^{2}}={{a}^{2}}$ is ${{x}^{2}}+{{y}^{2}}=2{{a}^{2}}$

Normal to the Circle 

The normal at any point $\text{P}$ of a curve is the straight line which passes through $\text{P}$ and is perpendicular to the tangent at $\text{P}$.

Generally, there is no need to remember the formula for normal to circle. We follow the following steps to find the equation of normal.

Step 1: Write the equation of tangent to the given circle at $\left( {{\text{x}}_{1}}{{\text{y}}_{1}} \right)$

Step 2: Write the equation of a line which is perpendicular to the above line & passes through $\left( {{\text{x}}_{1}}{{\text{y}}_{1}} \right)$. The equation obtained is the equation of normal.

To find the equation to the normal at the point $\left( {x}',{y}' \right)$ of $\left( 1 \right)$, the circle,

${{x}^{2}}+{{y}^{2}}={{a}^{2}}$

and (2), the circle

${{x}^{2}}+{{y}^{2}}+2gx+2fy+c=0$

(1) The tangent at $\left( \text{x'},\text{y'} \right)$ is

$x{x}'+y{y}'={{a}^{2}}$

i.e.

$y=\frac{{{x}'}}{{{y}'}}x+\frac{{{a}^{2}}}{{{y}'}}$

The equation to the straight line passing through $\left( \text{{x}'},\text{{y}'} \right)$ perpendicular to this tangent is,

$\text{y}-\text{{y}'}=\text{m}\left( \text{x}-\text{{x}'} \right)$

Where

$\text{m}\times \left( -\frac{{\text{{x}'}}}{{\text{{y}'}}} \right)=-1$

i.e.,

$\text{m}=\frac{{\text{{y}'}}}{{\text{{x}'}}}$

The required equation is therefore

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} y-{y}'=\frac{{{y}'}}{{{x}'}}\left( x-{x}' \right) \\ \text{ i}\text{.e}\text{., }~\text{ }{x}'y-x{y}'=0 \\ \end{array}$

Similarly you can find the equation of normal to the general form of circle.

Note: The equation of normal to circle ${{\text{x}}^{2}}+{{\text{y}}^{2}}={{\text{a}}^{2}}$ will always poss through centre and thus will be of the form $\text{y}=\text{mx}$
